title: LEVA: Using Large Language Models to Enhance Visual Analytics
venue: [llm_xai] IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
domain: LLMs for visual analytics workflows; explanation/summarization support
task: LEVA framework: use LLMs to support onboarding, exploration (insight recommendation), and summarization/reporting in VA systems.
abstract: Visual analytics supports data analysis tasks within complex domain problems. However, due to the richness of data types, visual designs, and interaction designs, users need to recall and process a significant amount of information when they visually analyze data. These challenges emphasize the need for more intelligent visual analytics methods. Large language models have demonstrated the ability to interpret various forms of textual data, offering the potential to facilitate intelligent support for visual analytics. We propose LEVA, a framework that uses large language models to enhance users’ VA workflows at multiple stages: onboarding, exploration, and arXiv:2403.05816v1 [cs.HC] 9 Mar 2024 summarization. To support onboarding, we use large language models to interpret visualization designs and view relationships based on system specifications. For exploration, we use large language models to recommend insights based on the analysis of system status and data to facilitate mixed-initiative exploration. For summarization, we present a selective reporting strategy to retrace analysis history through a stream visualization and generate insight reports with the help of large language models. We demonstrate how LEVA can be integrated into existing visual analytics systems. Two usage scenarios and a user study suggest that LEVA effectively aids users in conducting visual analytics.
explanation_techniques: LLM-generated explanations of visualization designs/relationships; insight suggestions; report generation
evaluation_design: System integration + user studies (mentioned as "Two usa..."; details later)
main_findings: Provides mixed-initiative exploration support and summarization via LLMs.
conclusions: LLMs can enhance VA workflows via guidance and explanation-like support.
limitations: PDF indicates arXiv preprint header; verify final TVCG version later.
notes: In-scope (LLM explanations/support in analytics), but verify published version. | PDF shows arXiv header; verify final published TVCG version before citing.

title: Investigating the Evolving Landscape of Deepfake Technology: Generative AI's Role in it's Generation and Detection
venue: [llm_xai] International Research Journal on Advanced Engineering Hub (IRJAEH)
task: Survey/overview of generative AI and deepfakes; implements deepfake image detection with ResNet-50 + MTCNN.
model: ResNet-50 + MTCNN for deepfake image detection (LLMs discussed only as background)
abstract: The world of artificial intelligence is constantly changing, with Generative AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) leading the way in bringing new technological advancements. This paper offers a detailed look at these groundbreaking technologies and how they are shaping the digital world today. We explore the technical aspects of Generative AI and LLMs, explain their unique features, and compare them to traditional AI models.One of the key focuses of our research is the growing issue of DeepFakes—artificial intelligence-generated media that presents a significant challenge in verifying content. We conduct a thorough examination of few deepfake detection techniques out of which we will be implementing and analyzing one of them. Our research implements a framework for Deep Fake Image Detection. The suggested solution utilizes a RESNET-50(Residual Network with 50 layers) and MTCNN (Multi-task Cascaded Convolutional Networks) models for detecting whether the images are real or fake. This study conducts the Hypothesis testing for the proposed solution taking in consideration that the current Deepfake detection algorithms are less effective in detecting highly realistic Deepfakes compared to less sophisticated manipulations. By investigating the convergence of deep learning, neural networks, and sophisticated algorithms, we set the stage for advancements in AI-based content verification. Keywords: AI-Based Content Verification; Artificial Intelligence; Deep Fake Image Detection; Deepfakes; Generative AI.
evaluation_design: Implementation report (details limited).
main_findings: Provides overview and implements a detection framework.
conclusions: Highlights deepfake risks and need for detection.
limitations: Not about LLM explainability; venue quality.
notes: Out-of-scope noise.

title: Interactive computer-aided diagnosis on medical image using large language models

abstract: Computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) has advanced medical image analysis, while large language models (LLMs) have shown potential in clinical applications. However, LLMs struggle to interpret medical images, which are critical for decision-making. Here we show a strategy integrating LLMs with CAD 1234567890():,; 1234567890():,; networks. The framework uses LLMs’ medical knowledge and reasoning to enhance CAD network outputs, such as diagnosis, lesion segmentation, and report generation, by summarizing information in natural language. The generated reports are of higher quality and can improve the performance of vision-based CAD models. In chest X-rays, an LLM using ChatGPT improved diagnosis performance by 16.42 percentage points compared to state-of-the-art models, while GPT-3 provided a 15.00 percentage point F1-score improvement. Our strategy allows accurate report generation and creates a patient-friendly interactive system, unlike conventional CAD systems only understood by professionals. This approach has the potential to revolutionize clinical decision-making and patient communication. title: How Is ChatGPT’s Behavior Changing Over Time?
domain: LLM evaluation drift; reproducibility; instruction-following changes
task: Evaluate March vs June 2023 versions of GPT-3.5/GPT-4 across tasks; document behavior/performance drift over time.
data_modality: text tasks (math, safety, opinion, QA, code) + visual reasoning
abstract: GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 are the two most widely used large language model (LLM) services. However, when and how these models are updated over time is opaque. Here, we evaluate the March 2023 and June 2023 versions of GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 on several diverse tasks: 1) math problems, 2) sensitive/dangerous questions, 3) opinion surveys, 4) multi-hop knowledge-intensive questions, 5) generating code, 6) U.S. Medical License tests, and 7) visual reasoning. We find that the performance and behavior of both GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 can vary greatly over time. For example, GPT-4 (March 2023) was reasonable at identifying prime vs. composite numbers (84% accuracy) but GPT-4 (June 2023) was poor on these same questions (51% accuracy). This is partly explained by a drop in GPT-4’s amenity to follow chain-of-thought prompting. Interestingly, GPT-3.5 was much better in June than in March in this task. GPT-4 became less willing to answer sensitive questions and opinion survey questions in June than in March. GPT-4 performed better at multi-hop questions in June than in March, while GPT-3.5’s performance dropped on this task. Both GPT-4 and GPT-3.5 had more formatting mistakes in code generation in June than in March. We provide evidence that GPT-4’s ability to follow user instructions has decreased over time, which is one common factor behind the many behavior drifts. Overall, our findings show that the behavior of the ‘same’ LLM service can change substantially in a relatively short amount of time, highlighting the need for continuous monitoring of LLMs. Keywords: GPT-4, ChatGPT, LLM drifts, temporal evaluation
explanation_techniques: Observes reduced amenity to chain-of-thought prompting as one factor; monitoring and evaluation methodology.
evaluation_design: Benchmark comparisons over time across multiple task suites.
main_findings: Shows substantial drift across tasks; decreased instruction-following may explain some drops.
conclusions: Continuous monitoring needed; results from "same" LLM service not stable over time.
limitations: Service-level black box; limited to two snapshots.
notes: In-scope as evaluation/reliability concern for explainability claims.

However, the original LLM-based ER systems face three low-quality problems in their generated explana- tions, i.e., lack of personalization, inconsistency, and ques- tionable explanation data. To address these problems, we pro- pose a novel LLM-based ER model denoted as LLM2ER to serve as a backbone and devise two innovative explainable quality reward models for fine-tuning such a backbone in a reinforcement learning paradigm, ultimately yielding a fine- tuned model denoted as LLM2ER-EQR, which can provide high-quality explanations. title: Evaluation and mitigation of the limitations of large language models in clinical decision-making
domain: clinical decision-making with LLMs; evaluation under realistic workflows; mitigation
task: Create MIMIC-based dataset (2,400 cases, abdominal pathologies) and framework simulating clinical workflow; evaluate and mitigate LLM limitations vs clinicians.
model: Llama 2 Chat 70B and derivatives (OASST, WizardLM, Clinical Camel, Meditron, etc.)
abstract: Clinical decision-making is one of the most impactful parts of a physician’s responsibilities and stands to benefit greatly from artificial intelligence solutions and large language models (LLMs) in particular. However, while LLMs have achieved excellent performance on medical licensing exams, these tests fail to assess many skills necessary for deployment in a realistic clinical decision-making environment, including gathering information, adhering to guidelines, and integrating into clinical workflows. Here we have created a curated dataset based on the Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care database spanning 2,400 real patient cases and four common abdominal pathologies as well as a framework to simulate a realistic clinical setting. We show that current state-of-the-art LLMs do not accurately diagnose patients across all pathologies (performing significantly worse than physicians), follow neither diagnostic nor treatment guidelines, and cannot interpret laboratory results, thus posing a serious risk to the health of patients. Furthermore, we move beyond diagnostic accuracy and demonstrate that they cannot be easily integrated into existing workflows because they often fail to follow instructions and are sensitive to both the quantity and order of information. Overall, our analysis reveals that LLMs are currently not ready for autonomous clinical decision-making while providing a dataset and framework to guide future studies. title: Evaluating and Enhancing Large Language Models’ Performance in Domain-Specific Medicine: Development and Usability Study With DocOA
domain: domain-specific medical LLM with RAG explainability (OA management) (JMIR preprint)
task: Benchmark framework for domain-specific medicine; develop DocOA (RAG+prompts) to identify evidence supporting answers.
abstract: Background: The efficacy of large language models (LLMs) in domain-specific medicine, particularly for managing complex diseases such as osteoarthritis (OA), remains largely unexplored. Objective: This study focused on evaluating and enhancing the clinical capabilities and explainability of LLMs in specific domains, using osteoarthritis (OA) management as a case study. Methods: A domain specific benchmark framework was developed, which evaluate LLMs across a spectrum from domain- specific knowledge to clinical applications in real-world clinical scenarios. DocOA, a specialized LLM designed for OA management integrating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and instructional prompts, was developed. It can identify the clinical evidence upon which its answers are based through RAG, thereby demonstrating the explainability of those answers. The study compared the performance of GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and a specialized assistant, DocOA, using objective and human evaluations. Results: Results showed that general LLMs like GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 were less effective in the specialized domain of OA management, particularly in providing personalized treatment recommendations. However, DocOA showed significant improvements. Conclusions: This study introduces a novel benchmark framework which assesses the domain-specific abilities of LLMs in multiple aspects, highlights the limitations of generalized LLMs in clinical contexts, and demonstrates the potential of tailored approaches for developing domain-specific medical LLMs. (JMIR Preprints 07/03/2024:58158) DOI: https://doi.org/10.2196/preprints.58158 Preprint Settings 1) Would you like to publish your submitted manuscript as preprint? title: Ethical Considerations and Fundamental Principles of Large Language Models in Medical Education: Viewpoint
domain: ethics and governance; transparency/interpretability in medical education (viewpoint)
task: Discuss ethical challenges and propose 8 principles for responsible LLM use in medical education (incl. transparency/interpretability).
abstract: This viewpoint article first explores the ethical challenges associated with the future application of large language models (LLMs) in the context of medical education. These challenges include not only ethical concerns related to the development of LLMs, such as artificial intelligence (AI) hallucinations, information bias, privacy and data risks, and deficiencies in terms of transparency and interpretability but also issues concerning the application of LLMs, including deficiencies in emotional intelligence, educational inequities, problems with academic integrity, and questions of responsibility and copyright ownership. This paper then analyzes existing AI-related legal and ethical frameworks and highlights their limitations with regard to the application of LLMs in the context of medical education. To ensure that LLMs are integrated in a responsible and safe manner, the authors recommend the development of a unified ethical framework that is specifically tailored for LLMs in this field. This framework should be based on 8 fundamental principles: quality control and supervision mechanisms; privacy and data protection; transparency and interpretability; fairness and equal treatment; academic integrity and moral norms; accountability and traceability; protection and respect for intellectual property; and the promotion of educational research and innovation. The authors further discuss specific measures that can be taken to implement these principles, thereby laying a solid foundation for the development of a comprehensive and actionable ethical framework. Such a unified ethical framework based on these 8 fundamental principles can provide clear guidance and support for the application of LLMs in the context of medical education. This approach can help establish a balance between technological advancement and ethical safeguards, thereby ensuring that medical education can progress without compromising the principles of fairness, justice, or patient safety and establishing a more equitable, safer, and more efficient environment for medical education. (J Med Internet Res 2024;26:e60083) doi: 10.2196/60083 KEYWORDS medical education; artificial intelligence; large language models; medical ethics; AI; LLMs; ethics; academic integrity; privacy and data risks; data security; data protection; intellectual property rights; educational research LLMs can be used to optimize the use of information and
explanation_techniques: transparency/interpretability as governance principle (non-technical)
evaluation_design: Viewpoint article.
main_findings: Identifies hallucinations, bias, privacy and academic integrity issues; proposes principles and measures.
conclusions: Calls for unified ethical framework for LLM use in medical education.
limitations: Non-technical.

title: Enhancing intention prediction and interpretability in service robots with LLM and KG
domain: Robotics; intent recognition; LLM+knowledge graph for interpretable reasoning
task: Human intention recognition/prediction for service robots in complex environments.
model: GPT-3.5 (LLM used for interpretation); KG + online reasoning graph (LKIRF).
data_modality: Human motion + environment observations; textual descriptions (transcripts/captions/object labels) (WHYACT dataset).
abstract: Proposes LKIRF, combining an offline knowledge graph with an online reasoning graph and an LLM to interpret observations and support intent recognition for service robots. Reports improved prediction accuracy versus baselines and improved transparency by making the reasoning chain explicit.
explanation_techniques: Knowledge graph–grounded reasoning; online reasoning graph; explicit reasoning trace/visualization of intent inference.
evaluation_design: Offline experiments on intent recognition/prediction; compares against baseline NLP reasoning/cosine-similarity models; reports Accuracy/F1/Precision/Recall.
main_findings: LLM alone underperforms baselines; adding KG reasoning improves F1 and accuracy and yields a more interpretable intent reasoning process.
conclusions: KG-grounded reasoning improves both generalization and interpretability for intent recognition; suggests richer KGs + human feedback updates as future work.
limitations: Evaluation uses datasets rather than real robot deployments; practical constraints (sensing, real-time) not fully addressed.

title: Diagnostic reasoning prompts reveal the potential for large language model interpretability in medicine
domain: Clinical LLMs; prompt-based interpretability; diagnostic reasoning
task: Study whether LLMs can mimic clinician diagnostic reasoning while retaining diagnostic accuracy.
data_modality: Clinical Q&A (modified MedQA/USMLE free-response); NEJM clinicopathologic case series.
abstract: Develops “diagnostic reasoning prompts” (differential diagnosis, intuitive/analytic reasoning, Bayesian inference) and compares them to chain-of-thought prompting on clinical diagnostic questions. Finds GPT-4 can be prompted to emulate common clinical reasoning processes without sacrificing diagnostic accuracy, offering an interpretable rationale clinicians can inspect.
explanation_techniques: Prompted rationales structured as clinical reasoning; differential diagnosis prompting; Bayesian-style prompts; rationale review for inaccuracies/false logic.
evaluation_design: Quantitative accuracy comparisons (GPT-3.5 vs GPT-4; CoT vs diagnostic reasoning prompts) + clinician review of rationales (blinded) for incorrect logic/inaccuracies.

title: Comparing human text classification performance and explainability with large language and machine learning models using eye-tracking
domain: Human-AI alignment; explainability comparison; eye-tracking; LLM prompting
task: Compare human vs ML vs LLM text classification performance and “explanation words” alignment.
model: ChatGPT-3.5; Logistic Regression + LIME; humans (non-expert) via eye-tracking
data_modality: Text (204 injury narratives); eye-tracking gaze traces
abstract: Empirical study comparing non-expert humans, a logistic-regression ML model, and ChatGPT-3.5 on classifying 204 domain-specific injury narratives into six cause-of-injury codes. Explainability is compared via top words: eye-tracking-derived attention for humans, LIME for the ML model, and prompt-elicited words for the LLM.
explanation_techniques: LIME feature attribution (LR); eye-tracking attention as human rationale proxy; prompt-based elicitation of LLM “top words”.
evaluation_design: User study with 51 participants (eye tracking) + ML model trained on 120k labeled narratives; zero-shot ChatGPT-3.5; compares accuracy by code and narrative complexity and compares overlap of top-k words.
main_findings: Trained LR outperforms zero-shot LLM and non-expert humans, especially for high-complexity narratives; top-3 predictive words show higher human–model agreement than later-ranked words.
conclusions: For domain-specific noisy text, a well-trained classical model can outperform non-expert humans and zero-shot LLMs; agreement-at-top-k suggests a practical way to quantify alignment between human attention and model explanations.
limitations: Small domain dataset; humans are non-experts; LLM explanations via prompts are not guaranteed faithful; word-overlap is a coarse proxy for reasoning alignment.

abstract: Proposes an automated, task-agnostic framework to evaluate LLM free-text rationales along two dimensions: fidelity (faithfulness/utility) and interpretability. title: Overlap in meaning is a stronger predictor of semantic activation in GPT-3 than in humans
domain: Interpretability/cognitive probing; semantic memory; comparing GPT-3 to humans
task: Probe GPT-3 semantic activation patterns using lexical decision task analogs; compare to human semantic priming data.
model: GPT-3 (and other LMs mentioned) using embedding cosine similarity as activation proxy
data_modality: Word pairs (semantic priming datasets) + embedding representations
abstract: Studies GPT-3 semantic activation by mapping lexical decision task semantic priming paradigms to embedding-space measures. Finds GPT-3 shows higher activation for related word pairs like humans, but its activation is more strongly predicted by semantic (meaning) similarity than associative co-occurrence, suggesting a different organization of semantic space.
explanation_techniques: No explanations; uses probing/analysis to interpret internal representations via activation proxies.
evaluation_design: Quantitative comparison of GPT-3 embedding-based activation against human semantic priming patterns across multiple analyses and association types.
main_findings: GPT-3 broadly mirrors human priming qualitatively but differs in that semantic similarity predicts activation more strongly than associative similarity.
conclusions: GPT-3 semantic network appears organized around meaning similarity more than co-occurrence, with implications for using LLMs as cognitive models and for interpretability analyses.
limitations: Activation measured indirectly via cosine similarity; does not test causal mechanisms; relevance to LLM-XAI is indirect (interpretability/probing rather than explanation generation).
notes: openalex_id: https://openalex.org/W4361204756 | oa_url: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-32248-6.pdf | pdf_url: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-32248-6.pdf

title: Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models
domain: Scientific discovery; LLM+search; interpretability via program search (background)
task: Use LLMs paired with evaluators to discover better programs/heuristics for open problems (cap set; online bin packing).
model: FunSearch (pre-trained LLM + evolutionary search + systematic evaluator)
data_modality: Programs/code as candidates; evaluator feedback scores
abstract: Introduces FunSearch, an evolutionary procedure pairing a pre-trained LLM with a systematic evaluator to search in function/program space, mitigating hallucinations by grounding search in evaluation. Demonstrates improved results on the cap set problem and new heuristics for online bin packing; argues discovered programs are more interpretable than raw solutions.
explanation_techniques: Not an explanation method; interpretability comes from producing explicit programs/heuristics that can be inspected and refined by experts.
evaluation_design: Empirical search experiments on mathematical/algorithmic benchmarks; compares achieved results to best-known baselines.
main_findings: LLM+evaluator program search can surpass best-known results and produce inspectable heuristics/programs.
conclusions: Grounded program search enables LLM-assisted discovery with feedback loops to domain experts; potentially useful for real-world algorithm design.
limitations: Not a survey XAI method paper; interpretability is indirect (program inspectability), not explanation faithfulness.
notes: openalex_id: https://openalex.org/W4389727268 | oa_url: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06924-6_reference.pdf | pdf_url: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06924-6_reference.pdf | PDF is “Accelerated Article Preview”/early typeset; verify final published version before citing.

evaluation_design: Empirical study using repeated ratings and statistical reliability analyses (e.g., agreement/consistency metrics).

title: Explaining Large Language Model-Based Neural Semantic Parsers (Student Abstract)
venue: [llm_xai] Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence
domain: LLM-based semantic parsing; local explanations; faithfulness vs plausibility (student abstract)
task: Explain predictions of LLM-based neural semantic parsers (text-to-SQL) and compare local explanation methods.
model: LLM-based semantic parser (UnifiedSKG mentioned); explanation methods compared
data_modality: Text questions + database schema; local perturbations; saliency/attribution maps
abstract: Student abstract studying local explanations for an LLM-based neural semantic parser, evaluating faithfulness (sufficiency/necessity) and plausibility. Reports Shapley-value methods (Kernel SHAP) and an LERG variant as relatively faithful compared to LIME and attention-based methods, and discusses costs and presentation challenges.
explanation_techniques: Local feature attribution: LIME, Kernel SHAP, attention, LERG variants; faithfulness via sufficiency/necessity; plausibility via manual inspection.
evaluation_design: Student abstract; small-scale manual study + faithfulness metrics on benchmark examples (Spider-like).
main_findings: Shapley-based methods and LERG-S show stronger faithfulness metrics; attention can be more faithful than LIME in their setup; highlights compute cost and feature interaction gaps.
conclusions: Need faithful yet efficient local explanations for LLM semantic parsers and better presentation for user understanding and debugging.
limitations: Student abstract (limited methodological detail); plausibility evaluation is qualitative; results may not generalize across parsers.

title: Evaluating large language models on a highly-specialized topic, radiation oncology physics
domain: Clinical domain benchmark; LLM evaluation; explanation-before-answer prompting
task: Evaluate LLMs on a highly specialized radiation oncology physics exam and test prompting for explanations/deductive reasoning.
data_modality: Expert-authored multiple-choice exam (100 questions); explanation prompts; repeated trials
abstract: Creates a 100-question radiation oncology physics exam and evaluates GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Bard (LaMDA), and BLOOMZ against medical physicists and non-experts. Tests “explain then answer” prompting, a novel deductive reasoning probe (“none of the above”), and majority-vote aggregation across trials.
explanation_techniques: Free-text explanations; “explain-then-answer” prompting; consistency across trials; deductive reasoning probe with “none of the above” substitution.
evaluation_design: Benchmark study: accuracy comparisons vs humans; repeated trials; prompt variants; majority vote analysis; deductive reasoning stress test.
main_findings: GPT-4 outperforms other LLMs and medical physicists on average; accuracy improves with explain-then-answer prompting; GPT models show high response consistency; human majority vote can outperform GPT-4.
conclusions: LLMs (especially GPT-4) show potential as knowledgeable assistants for specialized domains, but teams of experts still add value via aggregation.
limitations: Exam is author-constructed; specialization may bias results; explanation quality/faithfulness not guaranteed; model version drift.

title: Automated Paper Screening for Clinical Reviews Using Large Language Models: Data Analysis Study
domain: Systematic reviews; LLM-assisted screening; explainable include/exclude decisions
task: Use GPT/GPT-4 APIs to screen titles/abstracts for clinical reviews and compare against human ground truth.
data_modality: Titles/abstracts + natural-language inclusion/exclusion criteria; model decisions + rationales
abstract: Evaluates a workflow using GPT and GPT-4 APIs to screen over 24k titles/abstracts across six clinical review datasets using natural-language inclusion/exclusion criteria, comparing decisions to consensus labels from two human reviewers.
explanation_techniques: Prompted natural-language reasoning for include/exclude decisions; optional self-explanation and reflection prompts for errors.
evaluation_design: Large-scale retrospective evaluation; metrics include accuracy, macro-F1, sensitivity for included/excluded; agreement (kappa/PABAK) vs human consensus; runtime/cost reported.
main_findings: Reports high overall accuracy (~0.91) with included-paper sensitivity ~0.76 and excluded ~0.91; agreement with human consensus very high when adjusted (PABAK).

title: Augmenting interpretable models with large language models during training
domain: Interpretable models; LLM-assisted training; transparent inference
task: Use LLMs during training to build efficient, fully interpretable models for NLP tasks without LLM inference at runtime.
model: Aug-imodels: Aug-Linear (LLM-derived decoupled embeddings) and Aug-Tree (LLM keyphrase expansion) vs LLM baselines
data_modality: Text classification datasets; n-gram features; LLM embeddings/keyphrases
abstract: Proposes Aug-imodels, which leverage LLM knowledge during fitting to build extremely efficient and interpretable prediction models whose inference is transparent and >1000× smaller/faster than LLMs. Demonstrates Aug-Linear and Aug-Tree for NLP tasks.
explanation_techniques: Interpretable linear coefficients and keyphrase-expanded tree ensembles; instance-level feature contributions; transparency by design (no black-box at inference).
evaluation_design: Empirical evaluation on multiple NLP benchmarks with accuracy vs efficiency tradeoffs; analysis of fitted model coefficients and keyphrase expansions.
main_findings: LLM-augmented interpretable models can approach strong performance while remaining transparent and lightweight; large inference speed/memory gains vs LLMs.
conclusions: Use LLMs as teachers during training to produce deployable interpretable models suitable for high-stakes or compute-limited settings.
limitations: Limited to tasks where featureization works; depends on LLM quality during training; may not match LLM peak performance on all tasks.

title: Upstream Mitigation Is Not All You Need: Testing the Bias Transfer Hypothesis in Pre-Trained Language Models
venue: [llm_xai] Proceedings of the 60th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
domain: Fairness/bias; pre-trained LMs; bias transfer; mitigation evaluation
task: Test whether upstream debiasing of pre-trained LMs reduces downstream discriminatory behavior after fine-tuning.
data_modality: Text corpora + downstream classification datasets (e.g., bios, Wikipedia-based) + bias templates
abstract: Empirical study testing the “bias transfer hypothesis” that intrinsic social biases in pre-trained language models transfer into harmful downstream behavior after fine-tuning. Finds upstream debiasing interventions reduce intrinsic bias but do little to mitigate downstream disparities; downstream bias is better explained by fine-tuning dataset biases, though pre-training interacts with co-occurrence alterations.
explanation_techniques: Not XAI; focuses on bias measurement and mitigation (intrinsic vs extrinsic bias metrics; regression analyses).
evaluation_design: Controlled interventions pre-fine-tuning + downstream fine-tuning on two classification tasks; measures intrinsic bias (e.g., pronoun ranking / sentiment templates) and extrinsic harms (e.g., TPR gap); regression analysis of drivers.
main_findings: Upstream debiasing has limited effect on downstream discriminatory behavior; fine-tuning dataset bias dominates; simple downstream co-occurrence scrubbing may be ineffective when model is pre-trained.
conclusions: Practitioners should prioritize dataset quality and context-specific harms; upstream debiasing alone is insufficient.
limitations: Focused tasks/metrics; does not cover all harm types; does not provide open training code; results model/dataset dependent.
notes: openalex_id: https://openalex.org/W4285288507 | oa_url: https://aclanthology.org/2022.acl-long.247.pdf | pdf_url: https://aclanthology.org/2022.acl-long.247.pdf

title: SPoT: Better Frozen Model Adaptation through Soft Prompt Transfer
venue: [llm_xai] Proceedings of the 60th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
domain: Parameter-efficient adaptation; soft prompt tuning; transfer learning (background)
task: Improve prompt tuning by transferring learned soft prompts across tasks.
model: SPoT (Soft Prompt Transfer) over frozen pre-trained LMs (various sizes)
abstract: Proposes SPoT, a prompt-based transfer method that learns soft prompts on one or more source tasks and uses them to initialize prompt tuning for a target task, significantly boosting prompt-tuning performance. Reports that SPoT matches or outperforms full model fine-tuning on SuperGLUE while using far fewer task-specific parameters and studies task transferability at scale.
explanation_techniques: None (not explainability; training efficiency).
evaluation_design: Large-scale benchmark experiments (SuperGLUE; 26 tasks in 160 task-pairs) + prompt-as-task-embedding retrieval for selecting transferable sources.
main_findings: Soft prompt transfer markedly improves prompt tuning and can reach model-tuning performance with orders-of-magnitude fewer parameters.
conclusions: Prompt transfer enables parameter-efficient adaptation and supports scalable task selection via prompt embeddings.
limitations: Not an explainability method; relevance is indirect (enables adaptation of LLMs for later interpretability studies).

title: Logic Explained Networks
domain: Explainable AI; neural-symbolic explanations; logic constraints (non-LLM)
task: Provide human-understandable logical explanations for neural decisions via Logic Explained Networks (LENs).
data_modality: Generic tabular/image/text depending on use-case; concepts as intermediate representations
abstract: Introduces Logic Explained Networks, a family of neural models designed to produce explanations in a logical language that is both machine-implementable and human-understandable. LENs can operate directly on interpretable concepts or mimic a black-box classifier to provide an explanation-oriented module.
explanation_techniques: Built-in logical explanations; concept-based reasoning; optional distillation/mimicry of black-box models; supports supervised and unsupervised settings.
evaluation_design: Demonstrates LEN variants across multiple use cases (supervised classification focus; also unsupervised), comparing implementation choices and explanation customization.
main_findings: LENs generalize prior logic-based explanatory neural methods into a broader architecture family and allow customization of explanations/preferences.
conclusions: Mindful network design can integrate logic constraints/explanations into neural systems for broader XAI applicability.
limitations: Not about LLMs; extracted text indicates arXiv header in PDF; verify journal final version if citing; may rely on predefined concepts or auxiliary models.

title: General statistical model shows that macroevolutionary patterns and processes are consistent with Darwinian gradualism
task: Statistical model of directional and evolvability changes on phylogenies to explain macroevolutionary patterns.
model: Statistical phylogenetic model (directional shifts + evolvability changes)
data_modality: Phylogenetic trees + phenotypic traits (mammal body size)
abstract: Introduces a statistical model estimating both directional changes (mean shifts) and evolvability changes (trait-space exploration capacity) along phylogenetic trees, arguing macroevolutionary patterns like stasis and abrupt shifts can be reconciled with gradualist processes via biased random walks.
evaluation_design: Applied to mammalian body-size evolution with model comparison via marginal likelihoods and branch-wise effect estimates.
main_findings: Directional and evolvability effects independently explain macroevolutionary patterns; “watershed” evolvability increases outnumber reductions; abrupt shifts can be modeled statistically.
conclusions: Macroevolutionary theory benefits from models combining multiple processes; recasts phenomena in gradualist language.

title: ReFF: Reinforcing Format Faithfulness in Language Models Across Varied Tasks
venue: [llm_xai_v2] [llm_xai] Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence
domain: Format faithfulness; explanation/evaluation via programmatic checkers
task: Measure and improve LLM adherence to output formatting constraints (format faithfulness).
model: Multiple open/closed LLMs evaluated; FormatBench + ReFF (as described)
data_modality: Instructions/chat prompts; outputs validated by format checker programs
abstract: Introduces FormatBench, a benchmark for LLM format faithfulness across diverse tasks (NLP, creative, autonomous agents), interaction styles (single-turn, multi-turn), and format types (inclusion, wrapping, length, coding). Each task includes a decidable format checker; experiments show modern LLMs still frequently violate formatting constraints and motivates reinforcement methods (ReFF) for format faithfulness.
explanation_techniques: Not an explainer; uses automatic checkers as verifiable constraints (useful for auditable outputs).
evaluation_design: Benchmark evaluation with programmatic format checkers; compares models and (if included) training/finetuning variants.
main_findings: State-of-the-art LLMs show severe deficiencies in format faithfulness; checker-based evaluation exposes systematic failure modes.
conclusions: Decidable output formats allow rigorous auditing; improving format faithfulness supports safer LLM integration in downstream systems.
limitations: Formatting compliance does not guarantee semantic correctness; benchmark/task choices influence conclusions.

abstract: Introduces TalkToModel, a system for open-ended natural language dialogues to understand ML models on tabular datasets. An LLM parses user utterances into a structured query language; an execution engine runs multiple explanation/error analysis operations and can automatically select high-fidelity explanations to answer user questions.
explanation_techniques: Conversational interface for explanations; automatic explanation selection based on fidelity; supports counterfactuals, feature importance, subgroup analyses.
evaluation_design: Quantitative language-understanding evaluation on gold parses + human study with healthcare workers and ML practitioners comparing TalkToModel vs point-and-click explainability tools.
main_findings: Users prefer and are more effective with conversational explainability; system can generalize across domains via lightweight adaptation and supports multiple explanation types with fidelity-based selection.
conclusions: Natural language dialogues can make model explainability more accessible and actionable for diverse users and datasets.
limitations: Focused on tabular classifiers; depends on LLM parsing reliability; explanation selection fidelity depends on available metrics and operations.

abstract: Proposes a framework to evaluate clinical safety of LLM medical text summarization: an error taxonomy for hallucinations/omissions, an experimental structure for iterative pipeline comparison, a clinical safety framework for harm severity, and a GUI tool (CREOLA). Across 18 experimental configurations and 12,999 clinician-annotated sentences, reports ~1.47% hallucination and ~3.45% omission rates and shows prompt/workflow refinements can reduce major errors.
explanation_techniques: Error taxonomy + clinician-annotated auditing; traceable configurations; not an explanation method but an evaluation/safety framework.
evaluation_design: Clinician annotation of generated sentences; iterative comparisons across 18 pipeline configurations; safety scoring of errors; reports hallucination/omission rates.
main_findings: Provides operational taxonomy + tooling to quantify hallucinations/omissions and their clinical harm; major error rates can be reduced with workflow/prompt refinement.
conclusions: Clinical LLM summarization needs audit frameworks linking textual errors to patient-safety harms; taxonomy+GUI supports safer deployment.
limitations: Single evaluation setup; depends on annotation protocol; rates may vary by setting/model; conflicts noted (industry).

abstract: Reframes human-AI collaboration for explanation generation: prompt GPT-3 to overgenerate multiple free-text explanations per instance and train a supervised acceptability filter from human binary judgments to select plausible explanations. Shows acceptability correlates with fine-grained attributes and improves instance-level explanation quality compared to raw GPT-3 generations.
explanation_techniques: Overgenerate-and-filter pipeline; acceptability classifier; AMT head-to-head and absolute evaluation of explanation quality.
evaluation_design: Human subjects studies comparing GPT-3 explanations vs crowdsourced datasets; acceptability annotation; training acceptability classifiers and reporting AP/accuracy metrics.
main_findings: Humans often prefer GPT-3 explanations to crowdsourced ones, but raw generations are often unacceptable; acceptability filters can reliably select human-acceptable explanations.
conclusions: Prompting plus human-in-the-loop filtering is a practical way to scale free-text explanations while maintaining acceptability.
limitations: Acceptability subjective; filter requires labeled data; does not guarantee faithfulness to model reasoning.
