explanation_techniques: prompting techniques taxonomy (includes techniques often used to elicit rationales/explanations)
evaluation_design: narrative survey; categorization
main_findings: Provides practitioner-oriented taxonomy of prompt engineering methods with examples.
conclusions: Standardized categories may help navigate prompt technique landscape.
limitations: Secondary literature; not focused on explainability; unclear rigor/coverage.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=partial (prompting methods include explanation-elicitation variants).

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
task: overview of RAG for knowledge-grounded QA
abstract: Catchword/overview of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) as a framework to ground LLM outputs in retrieved knowledge to reduce hallucinations and improve trustworthiness for organizational data.
explanation_techniques: retrieved evidence/citations via RAG; grounded generation
evaluation_design: conceptual overview (no primary evaluation)
main_findings: Explains RAG pipeline, rationale, and why grounding helps mitigate hallucinations for enterprise/organizational contexts.
conclusions: RAG is a key practical approach for deploying LLMs over private/organizational knowledge bases with better reliability.
limitations: Not an empirical study; not focused on explainability evaluation.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=partial (grounding as explanation/evidence mechanism).

title: STELA: a community-centred approach to norm elicitation for AI alignment
task: norm elicitation for LLM alignment (participatory)
abstract: Introduces STELA, a community-centred process for eliciting norms/rules for aligning language-agent behavior, using deliberative focus groups with historically underrepresented communities and comparing derived rulesets to developer rulesets.
explanation_techniques: transparency via explicit rules/principles; participatory elicitation (not model explanations)
interaction_design: deliberative focus groups; community review; qualitative coding
evaluation_design: qualitative study + ruleset comparison
main_findings: Community-derived alignment priorities and harms differ from developer rulesets; participatory deliberation yields richer contextual insights for alignment.
conclusions: Community-centred norm elicitation is a valuable method to guide alignment of LLM-based dialogue systems.
limitations: Not an explainability method paper; context/US-specific samples; qualitative generalizability limits.
notes: in_scope=no (alignment/governance, not explainability in LLM outputs); still relevant for human-centered evaluation context.

title: Framework-based qualitative analysis of free responses of Large Language Models: Algorithmic fidelity
task: assess validity of LLM-simulated “silicon participants” for qualitative research
abstract: Defines and operationalizes “algorithmic fidelity” as validity for LLM-generated qualitative interview responses; compares themes and discourse properties between matched human participants and GPT-3.5 “silicon participants”.
explanation_techniques: validity/faithfulness concept (algorithmic fidelity); qualitative framework analysis
evaluation_design: paired qualitative analysis (themes + tone/structure) human vs silicon participants
main_findings: High-level themes can match, but tone/structure differ; evidence of hyper-accuracy distortions; GPT-3.5 insufficient algorithmic fidelity for generalizing to real populations.
conclusions: LLM-based qualitative research needs dedicated validity assessment; current models may not generalize across lived experiences.
limitations: Single model family; limited populations/tasks; not an explanation method study.
notes: in_scope=partial (faithfulness/validity framing relevant to explanation evaluation; not directly XAI).

title: Exploring the role of large language models in the scientific method: from hypothesis to discovery
task: review of LLM roles in scientific method
abstract: Perspective/review of how LLMs may support stages of the scientific method (hypothesis to discovery) and discusses prompting, RAG, agents, and evaluation considerations for AI4Science.
explanation_techniques: prompting/RAG/agent frameworks; not primarily explainability
evaluation_design: narrative review/perspective
main_findings: Maps LLM applications across scientific cycle; emphasizes need for clear evaluation metrics and human-aligned integration; discusses CoT and RAG as performance enablers.
conclusions: Deep integration with tools/agents plus robust evaluation is needed for LLMs to contribute reliably to scientific discovery.
limitations: Not focused on explainability; broad high-level claims.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (AI4Science review; not LLM explainability focus).

title: Fostering effective hybrid human-LLM reasoning and decision making
task: perspective on hybrid human-LLM reasoning
abstract: Perspective arguing for research focus on human–LLM interaction biases (hallucinations, inconsistency, sycophancy) and goals of mutual understanding and complementary team performance.
explanation_techniques: discussion of needs for mutual understanding/communication; not a concrete explainer
evaluation_design: conceptual perspective (no primary experiment)
main_findings: Identifies interaction-level biases and risks (overconfidence, algorithm aversion, prompt sensitivity) affecting decision making; proposes desiderata for collaboration research.
conclusions: Effective hybrid reasoning requires addressing interaction biases and designing workflows for complementary performance.
limitations: No empirical validation; not specific to explainability methods.
notes: in_scope=partial (reliance/interaction risks and evaluation framing relevant; not direct XAI).

title: ChatGPT as a COBUILD lexicographer
task: evaluate ChatGPT for dictionary entry generation
abstract: Evaluates ChatGPT-generated COBUILD-style dictionary entries (definitions and example sentences) against human COBUILD entries using blinded expert ratings; explores fine-tuning via expert feedback.
explanation_techniques: definitions as explanations of word meaning (lexicographic)
evaluation_design: expert evaluation (15 verbs; blinded randomized ratings)
main_findings: AI definitions comparable to human; examples and overall entries rated lower; suggests feedback could improve performance.
conclusions: LLMs may streamline lexicography but require quality control and improvement for example generation.
limitations: Small sample; domain-specific; not general LLM explainability.
notes: in_scope=no (lexicography; not LLM explainability).

title: Detecting AI-Generated Text: Factors Influencing Detectability with Current Methods
task: survey of AI-generated text detection methods
abstract: Survey of AI-generated text detection methods (watermarking, statistical/stylistic cues, ML classifiers), datasets, and factors influencing detectability under different scenarios.
explanation_techniques: detection rationales via statistical cues/features (varies); not XAI for LLM outputs
evaluation_design: survey; categorization + recommendations
main_findings: Detectability depends on model/decoding, length, domain, language, human editing, and adversarial obfuscation; summarizes methods and datasets.
conclusions: AIGT detection is moving-target; method selection must match scenario and threat model.
limitations: Secondary; includes preprints; not focused on explainability generation.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (detection/provenance, not explainability).

title: Evaluating Large Language Models in extracting cognitive exam dates and scores
task: extract cognitive exam dates/scores from clinical notes
abstract: Evaluates ChatGPT (GPT-4) and LLaMA-2 for extracting MMSE/CDR/MoCA exam dates and scores from 135k clinical notes; experts review outputs and report precision/recall/accuracy and error types including hallucinations.
explanation_techniques: error analysis; hallucination categorization (not explanations)
evaluation_design: retrospective validation with expert review; IRR + metrics
metrics_performance: precision/recall/accuracy; sensitivity; true-negative rates; Fleiss kappa
main_findings: GPT-4 outperforms LLaMA-2; LLaMA-2 shows more total hallucinations; task performance varies by exam (MMSE vs CDR).
conclusions: LLMs can support clinical information extraction but require careful validation and oversight; hallucinations remain a key risk.
limitations: Single health system; notes not shareable; limited fine-tuning; generalization unknown.
notes: in_scope=partial (clinical reliability + hallucination errors; not explainability).

title: STAR: Boosting Low-Resource Information Extraction by Structure-to-Text Data Generation with Large Language Models
task: low-resource event/relation extraction via synthetic data generation
model: LLM-assisted data generation + supervised IE models
abstract: Proposes STAR, a structure-to-text data generation method using LLMs to synthesize training instances from limited seed demonstrations for low-resource information extraction (event/relation extraction), with iterative self-reflection/refinement for quality.
explanation_techniques: self-reflection error identification; structured target generation (not user-facing explanations)
evaluation_design: benchmark experiments on low-resource event extraction and relation extraction; human assessment of generated data quality
main_findings: LLM-generated synthetic data significantly improves low-resource IE and can outperform human-curated data in effectiveness; human eval finds higher passage quality and better alignment with task definitions.
conclusions: LLM-based structured data generation is an effective approach for low-resource IE when designed with structure-first generation and refinement.
limitations: Not focused on explainability; depends on seed demonstrations and LLM quality; downstream model/setting specific.
notes: in_scope=no (IE data generation, not LLM explainability).

title: Computational Argumentation-based Chatbots: A Survey
task: survey of argumentation-based chatbots
model: argumentation frameworks + (mentions potential integration with LLMs)
abstract: Survey of chatbots that employ computational argumentation, reviewing how argumentation is integrated, benefits/drawbacks vs standard chatbots, and future directions including interaction with transformer/LLM architectures.
explanation_techniques: argument-based rationales; transparent reasoning via argumentation semantics
evaluation_design: survey; taxonomy/classification of systems
main_findings: Argumentation can improve coherence, strategy, and transparency of dialogue systems, though LLM-based chatbots currently outperform; suggests integration opportunities to address shortcomings like lack of explainability.
conclusions: Computational argumentation provides a promising path to more explainable and controllable chatbots and may complement LLM-based dialogue agents.
limitations: Secondary literature; not an empirical LLM explainability study.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=partial (argument-based explainability relevant, but not LLM explainability).

title: Digital forgetting in large language models: a survey of unlearning methods
task: survey of machine unlearning (“digital forgetting”) for LLMs
abstract: Survey of digital forgetting for LLMs via machine unlearning. Provides taxonomy of unlearning methods by reach/depth of model modifications, compares methods and evaluation (datasets/metrics), and outlines open problems and practitioner recommendations.
evaluation_design: survey; taxonomy + comparative review
main_findings: Unlearning must balance forgetting effectiveness, retained utility, and cost/scalability; surveys recent methods and evaluation practices for LLM unlearning.
conclusions: Dedicated unlearning techniques and standardized evaluation are needed for compliant and trustworthy LLM deployment.
limitations: Secondary; not focused on explainability generation.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (unlearning, not LLM explainability).

title: Making Natural Language Reasoning Explainable and Faithful
task: make multi-hop reasoning explainable and faithful via structured info
model: LLM + information extraction + semantic graphs (prompting); multi-module checking for inductive reasoning
abstract: Presents methods to improve explainability and faithfulness of natural-language reasoning by extracting structured information (semantic graphs) grounded in context and using it to guide step-wise reasoning; discusses modules and checks to reduce hallucinations and misalignment.
explanation_techniques: structured semantic graphs; step-wise grounded reasoning; iterative feedback/checking modules
evaluation_design: reported as talk/overview referencing prior work (e.g., EMNLP Findings 2023) and demonstrating improved faithfulness/accuracy when conditioning on extracted graphs
main_findings: Grounded semantic graph prompting supports more faithful reasoning chains and higher QA accuracy; modular checking can filter/refine inductive rules and reduce hallucinations.
conclusions: Constraining LLM reasoning with grounded structured representations can improve explainability and faithfulness compared to free-form chain-of-thought.
limitations: This PDF appears to be an extended abstract/talk; details and full experimental protocol may be in referenced paper(s).
notes: in_scope=yes; explanation_method=structured grounding; focus=faithfulness/reasoning.

title: AiGen-FoodReview: A Multimodal Dataset of Machine-Generated Restaurant Reviews and Images on Social Media
task: dataset + detection of machine-generated restaurant reviews/images
abstract: Introduces AiGen-FoodReview, a multimodal dataset of 20,144 authentic vs machine-generated restaurant review–image pairs generated with GPT-4-Turbo and DALL-E-2; evaluates unimodal and multimodal detection models and interpretable hand-crafted features.
explanation_techniques: interpretable feature scoring from readability/photographic theories (for detection)
evaluation_design: classification experiments; dataset construction; compare unimodal vs multimodal detectors
main_findings: Multimodal detection achieves very high accuracy; hand-crafted interpretable features are competitive and scalable.
conclusions: Synthetic multimodal content is detectable with strong models; dataset supports research on detection and analysis of LLM-generated reviews.
limitations: Not an explainability study; domain-specific (restaurants/Yelp/NYC).
notes: in_scope=no (AIGT detection/dataset; not LLM explainability).

title: Improving the Robustness of Knowledge-Grounded Dialogue via Contrastive Learning
task: robust knowledge-grounded dialogue under noisy context/KG
abstract: Proposes an entity-based contrastive learning framework to improve robustness of knowledge-grounded dialogue to real-world noise (misspellings/abbreviations; incomplete/erroneous/outdated KG facts) by constructing semantic-relevant and irrelevant perturbations using entity-guided augmentations.
explanation_techniques: knowledge grounding (KG); not focused on explanation
evaluation_design: experiments on three KGD benchmarks; robustness test set + few-shot and human evaluation
main_findings: Improves automatic metrics and robustness under noisy inputs; better responses in few-shot/noisy settings.
conclusions: Contrastive learning with entity-guided perturbations strengthens robustness of KGD systems for deployment.
limitations: Not explainability; KG noise simulation choices may affect results.
notes: in_scope=no (robustness for KGD; not LLM explainability).

title: Exploring the Potential for Large Language Models to Demonstrate Rational Probabilistic Beliefs
task: evaluate whether LLMs produce rational probabilistic beliefs
model: LLMs + uncertainty quantification prompting techniques; GPT-4o used for dataset generation
abstract: Introduces a dataset of indeterminate-truth claims (with complementary/specialised/generalised variants) and tests whether LLM probability assignments satisfy basic probabilistic constraints (complementarity and monotonicity), finding frequent violations even with uncertainty-quantification techniques.
explanation_techniques: probability/uncertainty estimates as model self-reported reasoning
evaluation_design: benchmark across models and prompting/UQ methods; checks violations of probabilistic axioms
main_findings: State-of-the-art LLMs often violate fundamental probability relationships; uncertainty estimates are not coherent/rational under tested methods.
conclusions: LLM probability outputs should not be assumed to reflect coherent probabilistic beliefs; calls into question claims of well-calibrated uncertainty.
limitations: Dataset generation uses LLM assistance and manual filtering; focuses on specific constraints rather than downstream tasks.
notes: in_scope=partial (uncertainty as explanation/reliability signal; not XAI methods per se).

title: A scientific-article key-insight extraction system based on multi-actor of fine-tuned open-source large language models
task: key-insight extraction from scientific articles
abstract: Develops ArticleLLM, an article-level key-insight extraction system using LLMs; improves performance via fine-tuning and proposes a multi-actor approach combining multiple fine-tuned models for better key-insight extraction.
evaluation_design: manual benchmark evaluation + fine-tuning; ensemble comparison
main_findings: LLM-based key-insight extraction is feasible; combining multiple fine-tuned models improves extraction performance and supports efficient literature review.
conclusions: Multi-actor cooperation among specialized LLMs can enhance document-level information extraction.
limitations: Not focused on explainability; domain/task-specific; dataset/benchmark details needed from full paper.
notes: in_scope=no (information extraction/summarization, not LLM explainability).

title: Integrating Algorithmic Decision Making into Small Business Credit Initiatives: a path to Enhanced Efficiency and Inclusive Economic Growth
task: conceptual framework for algorithmic decision making in SBCI credit evaluation
abstract: Proposes integrating algorithmic decision making, ML, and LLM-assisted document review into US Small Business Credit Initiatives evaluation workflows to improve efficiency and inclusivity; describes a high-level framework with feedback loops and monitoring.
explanation_techniques: mentions monitoring/ADM governance (no concrete explainer)
evaluation_design: conceptual paper (no clear empirical evaluation)
main_findings: Argues LLM-based document review + richer feature space could improve predictive power and adaptive thresholds; emphasizes monitoring and governance.
conclusions: ADM + LLMs may help credit initiatives if accompanied by robust monitoring and stakeholder feedback.
limitations: Low methodological detail; no empirical validation; venue appears non-mainstream.
notes: in_scope=no (not LLM explainability; credit ADM concept paper).

title: Explainable GENAI Techniques to Interpret and Visualize LLM Reasoning
venue: International Journal of Scientific Research in Engineering and Management (IJSREM)
task: framework to interpret/visualize LLM reasoning
abstract: Proposes an “explainable GenAI” framework combining retrieval-augmented generation and semantic attribution mapping with a visualization engine to present token-level reasoning traces as human-understandable narratives.
explanation_techniques: RAG grounding; attribution mapping; visualization of “reasoning traces”
evaluation_design: unclear/limited (appears conceptual; details not rigorous)
main_findings: Claims to decode and visualize contextual evidence influence on outputs; proposes visualization engine for transparency.
conclusions: Argues for integrated XAI+RAG+visualization approach for LLM trust.
limitations: Non-peer-reviewed/low-rigor venue; unclear experiments and metrics; potential overclaiming.
notes: in_scope=partial (claims about LLM reasoning visualization) but treat as low-quality/gray literature.

title: Suggestive answers strategy in human-chatbot interaction: a route to engaged critical decision making
task: interaction strategy (“suggestive answers”) to stimulate critical information seeking
abstract: Introduces a “suggestive ending” chatbot strategy (cliffhanger-like) that appends positive/negative hints instead of conclusions to stimulate curiosity and critical information seeking; evaluated via online user study (n=300) across decision-making tasks vs plain/expositive UIs.
explanation_techniques: UI-level prompting strategy; presenting contrasting perspectives (positive/negative)
interaction_design: between-subjects UI comparison: plain vs expositive vs suggestive endings
evaluation_design: online user study (between-subjects factorial); behavioral outcomes
main_findings: Suggestive chatbot users ask more questions, engage longer in information seeking, and form more multi-perspective opinions (effects depend on prior experience).
conclusions: Designing chatbot outputs to preserve “open loops” can reduce premature closure and encourage critical engagement.
limitations: Not an explainability method; generalization depends on task/UI and participant sample.
notes: in_scope=partial (human-facing explanation/interaction & overreliance risks).

title: An Enhanced Textual Review Classification and Sentiment Analysis Approach based on Machine Learning: A Comprehensive Analysis for Text Categorization Approaches
venue: The Asian Bulletin of Big Data Management
task: overview of sentiment/text classification and XAI methods; includes simple ML experiments
abstract: Discusses evolution of sentiment analysis from feature-engineered ML to transformers/LLMs and summarizes explainable NLP approaches (attribution, rationalization); reports very high accuracies on review datasets using an ML classifier.
explanation_techniques: feature attribution (LIME/IG/SHAP) + rationalization (surveyed)
evaluation_design: narrative overview + small reported experiments
main_findings: Presents taxonomy of subjective tasks and discusses explainability methods; claims high accuracy on UCI-ML reviews and Twitter Kaggle datasets.
conclusions: Highlights importance of feature extraction and XAI for subjective NLP; provides overview for practitioners.
limitations: Non-mainstream venue; unclear experimental rigor; broad claims; not LLM explainability-focused.
notes: in_scope=no (general sentiment/XAI overview; low relevance to LLM explainability).

title: A Review of Image-Based Simulation Applications in High-Value Manufacturing
venue: Archives of Computational Methods in Engineering
task: survey of image-based simulation (IBSim) in high-value manufacturing
abstract: Survey of image-based simulation workflows that build simulation-ready geometries from imaging data (e.g., CT) for part-specific simulations in high-value manufacturing; reviews applications and future trends.
evaluation_design: review article
main_findings: Summarizes IBSim workflow stages and applications across materials and manufacturing techniques.
conclusions: IBSim enables more accurate simulations when as-manufactured geometry deviates from CAD, with growing relevance as imaging becomes widespread.
limitations: Not related to LLMs or explainability.
notes: in_scope=no (not LLM/XAI topic; likely included by keyword noise).

title: OntoFact: Unveiling Fantastic Fact-Skeleton of LLMs via Ontology-Driven Reinforcement Learning
task: mine missing/unknown facts and reveal ontology-level patterns of factual errors
abstract: Proposes OntoFact, an ontology-driven framework to detect missing/unknown facts in LLMs by adaptively generating error-prone KG-derived test cases via ontology-driven reinforcement learning and analyzing ontology-level “fact skeletons”; includes a hallucination-free detection strategy to reduce bias.
explanation_techniques: structured KG probes; ontology-level analysis; hallucination-free detection strategy
evaluation_design: benchmarking on 5 KGs/datasets across 32 LLMs; error-rate analysis
main_findings: Finds substantial factual errors and Yes-bias; reports high fact error rates for ChatGPT on DBpedia/YAGO; provides open benchmarks and method to mine error patterns.
conclusions: Current LLMs exhibit broad factual gaps and biases; ontology-guided probing improves coverage and robustness of factuality evaluation.
limitations: Focuses on factuality detection rather than explanations; relies on KG quality and QA prompting.
notes: in_scope=partial (structured probing + error pattern analysis relevant to reliability/faithfulness).

title: Toward practical human-interpretable explanations
task: human-interpretable feature attribution without invertible mapping
abstract: Introduces Latent SHAP, a model-agnostic black-box feature attribution framework that produces human-interpretable explanations without requiring an invertible transformation from raw features to interpretable concepts; evaluates faithfulness on controlled datasets and demonstrates utility across domains including NLP/LLMs.
explanation_techniques: Latent SHAP (attribution in interpretable space via non-invertible transform)
evaluation_design: quantitative faithfulness on controlled datasets + case studies
main_findings: Enables practical human-interpretable explanations when only non-invertible mapping to interpretable features exists; shows competitive faithfulness in controlled setups.
conclusions: Bridges gap between popular attribution methods and human-interpretable representations in real-world settings.
limitations: Not specific to LLM explainability; depends on quality of interpretable representation and transform.
notes: in_scope=partial (relevant XAI method; not LLM-specific).

title: A survey on large language model based autonomous agents
task: survey of LLM-based autonomous agents (construction/applications/evaluation)
abstract: Survey of LLM-based autonomous agents, proposing a unified framework for agent construction (architecture, memory, planning, tools), summarizing applications, and discussing evaluation strategies, challenges, and future directions.
explanation_techniques: mentions natural-language interfaces as “more flexible and explainable” (high-level)
evaluation_design: survey
main_findings: Systematizes agent components (profiles, memory modules, planning) and evaluation practices; identifies open challenges.
conclusions: LLM agents benefit from world knowledge and tool integration but need better evaluation and robustness.
limitations: Secondary literature; not focused on explainability methods.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (agents survey; not explainability focus).

title: The GenAI is out of the bottle: generative artificial intelligence from a business model innovation perspective
task: scoping review of generative AI impacts on business model innovation
abstract: Scoping review of generative AI (e.g., ChatGPT, Jasper, DALL-E) from a business model innovation perspective; develops propositions on impacts and discusses industry examples (software engineering, healthcare, financial services) using qualitative content analysis over 513 data points.
evaluation_design: scoping review / qualitative content analysis
main_findings: Synthesizes implications of GenAI for business model innovation and proposes propositions and examples across industries.
conclusions: GenAI can reshape business models; organizations need to understand capabilities/limitations and integrate responsibly.
limitations: Not focused on explainability; broad management perspective.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (management/GenAI, not LLM explainability).

title: 14 examples of how LLMs can transform materials science and chemistry: a reflection on a large language model hackathon
task: examples/prototypes of LLM use in materials/chemistry workflows
abstract: Perspective reflecting on a large language model hackathon and presenting 14 prototype examples of how LLMs can aid materials science and chemistry (interfaces, extraction from unstructured text, education, molecule discovery).
explanation_techniques: tooling/interfaces; not dedicated explainability
evaluation_design: hackathon reflection; prototypes (little/no evaluation)
main_findings: Demonstrates breadth of possible LLM-enabled tools and workflows; provides links to prototype repositories.
conclusions: LLMs likely to impact scientific workflows substantially; prototyping is fast but rigorous evaluation remains open.
limitations: Not an empirical evaluation; primarily inspirational examples.
notes: in_scope=no (AI4Science applications; not explainability focus).

title: Generative artificial intelligence
task: overview of generative AI fundamentals, properties, potentials, challenges
abstract: Provides an overview of generative AI fundamentals, terms/techniques, properties, and discusses potentials and challenges; emphasizes understanding distinctive characteristics to harness benefits and mitigate risks.
evaluation_design: conceptual overview
main_findings: Summarizes generative AI concepts and key challenges/risks at a high level.
conclusions: Researchers/practitioners should understand generative AI properties for responsible use.
limitations: Not LLM explainability-focused.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (general GenAI overview).

title: Overview of Chatbots with special emphasis on artificial intelligence-enabled ChatGPT in medical science
task: review of chatbots and ChatGPT in medical science
abstract: Review of chatbots with emphasis on ChatGPT in medicine: evolution/architecture/medical uses of chatbots; ChatGPT architecture/training, diagnosis/treatment use cases, ethical issues, comparison to other NLP models, limitations and prospects.
explanation_techniques: mentions ethics/limitations; no concrete XAI method
evaluation_design: narrative review
main_findings: Summarizes current uses and potential of ChatGPT/LLMs in healthcare alongside risks and ethical concerns.
conclusions: LLMs have promise in healthcare but require more research, validation, and governance.
limitations: Secondary; broad scope.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (medical chatbot review; not explainability focus).

title: Enhancing Job Recommendation through LLM-Based Generative Adversarial Networks
task: job recommendation with LLM-based GAN approach
abstract: Proposes a job recommendation method leveraging LLMs and a generative adversarial setup to mitigate low-quality/incomplete resumes and improve matching, aiming for higher practical effectiveness.
evaluation_design: offline experiments (details in paper)
main_findings: Uses LLM knowledge/reasoning to enhance job recommendation under noisy resume data.
conclusions: LLM-augmented recommendation can improve robustness to resume quality issues.
limitations: Not explainability; domain-specific.
notes: in_scope=no (recommender system, not explainability).

title: ChatGPT: deconstructing the debate and moving it forward
task: philosophical analysis of authorship/deception debates around ChatGPT
abstract: Analyzes ethical debates about ChatGPT (authorship, deception) focusing on assumptions about authorship/language and the appearance–reality distinction; offers an alternative framing via deconstruction and addresses counter-objections.
evaluation_design: philosophical analysis
main_findings: Argues for alternative conceptualization of what goes on with ChatGPT that avoids appearance/reality framing; discusses ethical implications.
conclusions: Debates benefit from clearer philosophical assumptions and alternative framing.
limitations: Not about explainability methods.
notes: in_scope=no (ethics/philosophy, not LLM explainability).

title: How to DP-fy ML: A Practical Guide to Machine Learning with Differential Privacy
task: practical guide to differential privacy for ML
abstract: Practical guide for training ML models with differential privacy, covering what DP protection entails, target guarantees, and strategies for privacy–utility–compute tradeoffs, including hyperparameter tuning and architectural considerations.
evaluation_design: tutorial/guide
main_findings: Synthesizes practical DP ML guidance and tuning tricks for broader adoption.
conclusions: DP ML is feasible with careful choices and tuning; aims to facilitate deployment.
limitations: Not LLM explainability-focused.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (differential privacy guide).

title: DesignPrompt: Using Multimodal Interaction for Design Exploration with Generative AI
task: multimodal prompt authoring for text-to-image GenAI
model: text-to-image GenAI (tool supports multimodal prompts; underlying model not central)
abstract: Introduces DesignPrompt, a moodboard tool that lets designers compose multimodal prompts (images, colors, semantics, text) and iteratively refine them; evaluated via structured observation study with 12 professional designers vs text-only prompting, focusing on intent expression, expectation alignment, and perceived transparency.
explanation_techniques: interaction to improve intent expression and transparency perception (not model explanations)
interaction_design: moodboard-based multimodal prompt composition + iterative refinement
evaluation_design: comparative structured observation study
main_findings: Multimodal prompts help designers express intentions and explore; interaction preferences depend on sense of control and goal (inspiration vs specific image).
conclusions: Designer-centered multimodal prompting tools can better align GenAI interaction with design thinking.
limitations: Not a model explainability method; small sample; results tied to design context.
notes: in_scope=partial (human-facing transparency and promptability for GenAI).

title: An overview of large AI models and their applications
task: review of large AI models (LLMs/LVMs/multimodal) and applications
abstract: Review of large-scale AI models and their algorithmic principles and industrial applications; discusses evolution, core technologies, limitations (compute, privacy), and future trends including cross-modal fusion.
evaluation_design: review
main_findings: Summarizes development history and challenges of scaling, privacy, and multimodal limitations.
conclusions: Model scale likely to expand further; cross-modal fusion is a key trend.
limitations: Not explainability-focused; broad overview.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (general large model overview).

title: How should we change teaching and assessment in response to increasingly powerful generative Artificial Intelligence? Outcomes of the ChatGPT teacher survey
task: survey on how teaching/assessment should change due to ChatGPT
abstract: Mixed-methods teacher survey (n=318) on perceived impact of generative AI (ChatGPT) on teaching/assessment and how practices should change; identifies curriculum/pedagogy/assessment themes and motivations.
evaluation_design: mixed methods (quant + thematic analysis)
main_findings: Most teachers expect major impact; awareness predicts perceived impact; themes include learning with AI, higher-order thinking, ethics, process-focused assessment, and relational learning.
conclusions: Education systems should adapt pedagogy and assessment to generative AI while emphasizing ethics and higher-order skills.
limitations: Not about explainability methods; survey context dependent.
notes: in_scope=no (education survey; not LLM explainability).

title: Prompt to Transfer: Sim-to-Real Transfer for Traffic Signal Control with Prompt Learning
task: sim-to-real transfer for traffic signal control using prompt learning
model: RL policy + grounded action transformation + LLM prompting (PromptGAT)
abstract: Proposes PromptGAT, integrating LLM knowledge about dynamics changes (weather/road) via prompt-based dynamics profiling into grounded action transformation for sim-to-real RL in traffic signal control, reducing performance gaps.
explanation_techniques: LLM-derived dynamics profiling via prompts (not XAI)
evaluation_design: experiments on 4 scenarios in sim-to-sim transfer setting
main_findings: LLM-informed dynamics profiling improves action transformation and mitigates sim-to-real performance gaps.
conclusions: Prompting LLMs for domain knowledge can help adapt RL policies across dynamic shifts.
limitations: Not explainability; depends on LLM reliability; evaluated in simulation-to-simulation.
notes: in_scope=no (RL transfer, not LLM explainability).

title: Exploring the Darkverse: A Multi-Perspective Analysis of the Negative Societal Impacts of the Metaverse
task: multi-perspective analysis of negative societal impacts of metaverse
abstract: Multi-perspective paper compiling expert narratives on negative societal impacts (“darkverse”) of the metaverse (privacy, crime, misinformation, mental health, harassment) and synthesizing themes and propositions.
evaluation_design: expert perspectives synthesis
main_findings: Catalogues risk themes and proposes implications for policy/practice.
conclusions: Metaverse risks need proactive governance and mitigation strategies.
limitations: Not related to LLM explainability.
notes: in_scope=no (metaverse risks; out-of-scope).

title: Value Kaleidoscope: Engaging AI with Pluralistic Human Values, Rights, and Duties
task: model pluralistic values/rights/duties and explain decision variability
model: Kaleido (structured multi-task model) distilled from GPT-4; ValuePrism dataset
abstract: Introduces ValuePrism dataset (218k values/rights/duties linked to 31k situations) generated by GPT-4 and validated by humans, and Kaleido, a lightweight structured model to generate, explain, and assess relevance/valence of values to reflect value pluralism.
explanation_techniques: value-based explanations; structured modular generation + relevance/valence + explanations; contrasting values
evaluation_design: dataset creation + human evaluation + model comparison vs GPT-4 teacher; transfer to other datasets
main_findings: Humans prefer Kaleido outputs over GPT-4 teacher for accuracy/coverage; helps explain variability by contrasting values; modular interpretable approach transfers.
conclusions: Explicit structured modeling can better capture pluralistic human values and support explainable, value-aware AI.
limitations: Focus on value modeling rather than LLM explanation; dataset generation depends on GPT-4 and annotator demographics.
notes: in_scope=partial (structured explanations and contrasting values relevant to XAI/human factors).

title: Psychologically-Valid Generative Agents: A Novel Approach to Agent-Based Modeling in Social Sciences
task: framework for psychologically-valid generative agents in ABM
model: cognitive architecture + LLM for language + stance detection initialization
abstract: Proposes Psychologically-Valid Generative Agents combining a cognitive architecture for decision-making with an LLM for human-like linguistic output, plus stance detection to initialize agents from real-world data, aiming for more realistic ABM simulations.
explanation_techniques: cognitive-architecture grounding; explicit mechanisms vs pure LLM agents
evaluation_design: framework/proposal with discussion of prior component results
main_findings: Argues hybrid architecture can yield more realistic behavior than LLM-only generative agents and scale to population models.
conclusions: Combining cognitive architectures with LLM language generation and NLP initialization may improve ABM realism.
limitations: Not a direct explainability method; evaluation details limited in this paper.
notes: in_scope=no (agents/ABM; not LLM explainability focus).

title: Exploring prospects, hurdles, and road ahead for generative artificial intelligence in orthopedic education and training
task: narrative review of generative AI in orthopedic education
abstract: Narrative review of generative AI in orthopedic education/training: potential applications (real-time explanations, adaptive materials, simulations) and challenges (accuracy, bias, inconsistency, ethics/regulation, oversight), and future research directions.
explanation_techniques: mentions “real-time explanations” as application (educational), not XAI research
evaluation_design: narrative review
main_findings: Generative AI can support personalized, interactive learning but current performance on orthopedic exams is suboptimal; oversight is critical.
conclusions: Need optimized medical-content models, ethical best practices, curriculum integration, and long-term outcome evaluation.
limitations: Secondary; not XAI-specific.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (medical education review).

title: How AI tools can—and cannot—help organizations become more ethical
task: argument on how AI tools can support ethical upskilling
abstract: Argues AI systems will not be more ethical than humans; responsibility remains human. Proposes using AI as a “mirror” (bias/interpretability/counterfactual modeling) to ethically upskill leaders by understanding psychological underpinnings of unethical behavior.
explanation_techniques: interpretability + counterfactual modeling as tools for reflection
evaluation_design: conceptual perspective
main_findings: Positions AI as support for ethical learning rather than moral agent; proposes collaborative paradigm human+AI.
conclusions: AI can aid ethical decision-making only if humans develop ethical maturity and use interpretability insights.
limitations: Not LLM explainability; no empirical evaluation.
notes: in_scope=no (org ethics perspective; not LLM explainability).

title: Medical reasoning in LLMs: an in-depth analysis of DeepSeek R1
task: analyze LLM medical reasoning vs expert patterns
abstract: Evaluates DeepSeek R1 medical reasoning on 100 MedQA clinical cases; reports diagnostic accuracy and analyzes reasoning patterns and error modes, relating reasoning length to accuracy as a confidence proxy.
explanation_techniques: explicit reasoning steps; qualitative error taxonomy; reasoning-length heuristic as uncertainty signal
evaluation_design: qualitative + quantitative analysis of 100 MedQA cases
metrics_performance: diagnostic accuracy; reasoning length; error categories
main_findings: Achieves 93% diagnostic accuracy; errors include anchoring, conflict integration issues, limited alternatives, overthinking, incomplete knowledge; longer reasoning correlates with higher error probability.
conclusions: Reasoning traces can resemble clinical reasoning but require calibration; reasoning length may be a practical red flag for uncertainty/rationalization.
limitations: Single model and dataset slice; alignment with true expert reasoning remains partial; reasoning length heuristic may be brittle.
notes: in_scope=yes (medical reasoning/explainability evaluation; overthinking vs correctness).

title: Prospects of artificial intelligence in the development of sustainable separation processes
task: perspective on AI for sustainable separation processes
abstract: Perspective on how AI/ML (including emerging GPT/LLM tools) can support sustainable separation processes via property prediction, material discovery, process optimization, and technique selection; discusses history, benefits, limitations, and future vision.
evaluation_design: perspective
main_findings: Highlights opportunities for data-driven optimization and accelerated materials screening; notes limitations (data, compute, deployment).
conclusions: AI can contribute to sustainable separations but requires data, validation, and integration with domain expertise.
limitations: Not an explainability paper; broad overview.
notes: in_scope=no (domain perspective; not LLM explainability).

title: There and Back Again: Extracting Formal Domains for Controllable Neurosymbolic Story Authoring
task: hybrid LM + planner story authoring; extract planning domains from text
abstract: Explores hybrid approaches combining language models with narrative planners: uses GPT-4 to extract PDDL story domains/problems from natural language stories with minimal human intervention, and uses logical plans to generate stories that respect planner commitments.
explanation_techniques: planner-based causal/intentional explanations; controllability and interpretability via symbolic plans
evaluation_design: system/prototype demonstration
main_findings: Shows bidirectional pipeline between natural language stories and formal planning domains; aims for human-centric authoring with controllability.
conclusions: Neurosymbolic hybridization can improve controllability/explainability vs pure LM story generation.
limitations: Not LLM explainability for decision-making; creative-domain specific; limited evaluation.
notes: in_scope=partial (uses explicit claim about lack of explainability; symbolic plans as interpretability).

title: Leveraging ChatGPT and explainable AI for enhancing clinical decision support
task: LLM-based tabular clinical decision making with explanation-informed prompts
model: LLM (via prompting) + AutoML models + XAI (local explanations)
abstract: Presents HealthAI-Prompt: adapts LLMs for tabular clinical decision making (diabetes risk) via contextual prompts combining task description, domain knowledge from high-performing AutoML models, and selected local explanations evaluated for fidelity/stability/monotonicity; compares prompt strategies and baselines incl. chain-of-thought.
explanation_techniques: explanation-informed prompts; selection of local explanations based on fidelity/stability/monotonicity; AutoML-derived insights; CoT baseline
evaluation_design: comparative experiments of prompting strategies vs AutoML under diverse data conditions; explanation metric evaluation
main_findings: Embedding vetted local explanations into prompts helps LLM interpret structured features without fine-tuning and improves transparency; studies impact of prompt choices on performance.
conclusions: Bridging AutoML + explanation selection + prompting can improve LLM usability for tabular clinical prediction.
limitations: Task-specific (diabetes); relies on quality of explanations and prompt design; clinical validity requires further study.
notes: in_scope=yes (clinical LLM explainability on tabular data; uses explicit XAI metrics).

title: A prompt framework for enhancing LLM-based explainability of medical machine learning models: an intensive care unit application
venue: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
task: LLM-generated narrative explanations from SHAP outputs
model: GPT-4 + SHAP (extubation failure model on MIMIC-III)
abstract: Proposes and evaluates a structured zero-shot prompting framework to guide GPT-4 in generating consistent, clinically relevant narratives from SHAP values for an ICU extubation failure model; evaluates consistency (entropy/accuracy/fidelity) and clinician survey.
explanation_techniques: prompted explanation of SHAP; structured 3-step reasoning (risk factors, reconciliation, recommendations) with safety principles
evaluation_design: repeat prompting (10 generations per case; 40 cases) + clinician survey
main_findings: Achieves fidelity 0.783 and low entropy 0.226; clinicians rate explanations helpful (~3.94/5) and safe (~4.21/5) with lower ratings by critical care specialists.
conclusions: Structured prompting can translate SHAP into more usable clinical narratives; feasibility shown for SHAP-to-LLM pipeline.
limitations: Exploratory; small clinician sample; single model/task; risks of hallucination mitigated but not eliminated.
notes: in_scope=yes (LLM-assisted explanation of XAI in clinical setting).

title: Embedding Search for Quranic Texts based on Large Language Models
venue: The International Arab Journal of Information Technology
task: semantic search over Quran verses using embeddings
model: LLM embeddings (GPT-3.5 mentioned) vs AraT5 baseline
abstract: Investigates semantic search for Quranic texts using LLM embeddings and compares performance against a baseline embedding method across queries of varying semantic complexity; finds LLM embeddings effective, while AraT5 performs better on low-level semantic queries.
evaluation_design: retrieval evaluation over query sets with varying semantic levels
main_findings: LLM embeddings capture deep semantic connections consistently across complexity; suggests fine-tuning LLMs on Arabic corpora for further gains.
conclusions: Embedding-based semantic search with LLMs is promising for Quranic text retrieval.
limitations: Not explainability; task is retrieval; details of metrics/datasets needed for reproducibility.
notes: in_scope=no (semantic search; not explainability).

title: Artificial intelligence in personalized nutrition and food manufacturing: a comprehensive review of methods, applications, and future directions
task: review of AI methods for personalized nutrition and food manufacturing
abstract: Comprehensive review of AI-driven personalized nutrition (biosensors, ML/RL, CV dietary assessment) and AI in food manufacturing (quality control, predictive maintenance, waste minimization), discussing transparency, privacy, and equitable access challenges.
explanation_techniques: mentions explainable AI and symbolic extraction for transparency (high-level)
evaluation_design: review
main_findings: AI enables dynamic individualized diet recommendations and smart manufacturing but faces transparency and ethics barriers.
conclusions: Future work should address explainability, validation, and equitable deployment.
limitations: Not LLM explainability-focused.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no (broad nutrition review).

title: Generative AI meets CAD: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models
venue: The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology
task: LLM-assisted CAD model generation and refinement
model: GPT-4o + OpenSCAD + computer vision + feedback loops
abstract: Presents a framework (GAD) for LLM-assisted CAD: GPT-4o processes multimodal user inputs (text/voice/images) to generate OpenSCAD code, with syntax checks, self-evaluation and user feedback loops to iteratively refine 3D models and export to manufacturing formats.
explanation_techniques: human-in-the-loop feedback loops; not focused on explanation
evaluation_design: system/framework description with testing/validation loops
main_findings: Suggests LLMs can lower barriers to CAD and support design-to-manufacturing workflows via iterative refinement.
conclusions: Generative AI can simplify CAD modeling and enable more accessible design automation.
limitations: Not explainability; effectiveness depends on prompt/vision reliability and validation; evaluation details unclear.
notes: in_scope=no (CAD automation; not explainability).

title: Explainable AI In Software Engineering: Enhancing Developer-AI Collaboration
venue: The American Journal of Engineering and Technology
task: conceptual framework for XAI in software engineering workflows
model: AI tools for code tasks + explanation engines
abstract: Discusses integrating explainable AI into software engineering (code generation/defect prediction/planning) to support a developer-in-the-loop paradigm; proposes modular architecture integrating explanation engines with IDEs/dashboards/CI; includes a brief case study on explainable code review.
explanation_techniques: XAI for code review/defect prediction (high-level); human-AI dialogue mechanisms (proposed)
evaluation_design: conceptual + illustrative case study
main_findings: Argues transparency improves trust and team learning; proposes future directions like personalization and cross-SDLC integration.
conclusions: XAI is positioned as essential for reliable AI-assisted development environments.
limitations: Low-rigor/non-mainstream venue; unclear empirical evidence.
notes: in_scope=partial (XAI framing, but weak evidence/venue).

title: Encountering Artificial Intelligence: Ethical and Anthropological Investigations
venue: Dicastery for Culture and Education (Holy See) / Pickwick Publications (edited volume)
task: edited volume on ethical and anthropological investigations of AI
abstract: Edited volume collecting theological/ethical reflections on artificial intelligence from Catholic social and moral thought; chapters cover approaches to AI ethics and anthropological questions about AI and society.
evaluation_design: edited volume
main_findings: Normative framing of AI ethics and human responsibility; not an empirical LLM study.
conclusions: Provides moral-theological perspectives on AI development and use.
limitations: Not LLM explainability; not empirical.
notes: in_scope=no (ethics volume; not LLM explainability).

title: Educational futures of intelligent synergies between humans, digital twins, avatars, and robots - the iSTAR framework
task: framework for human-machine synergy in teaching
abstract: Proposes iSTAR framework (Intelligent human-machine Synergy in collaborative teaching) modeling collaboration among humans, digital twins, avatars/agents and robots; introduces DELTA dimensions (design, ethics, learning, teaching, assessments) for safe and responsible implementation.
evaluation_design: framework paper
main_findings: Provides dimensions and ecosystem view for future human-machine teaming in education.
conclusions: Calls for structured consideration of design/ethics/assessment in AI-augmented teaching.
limitations: Not LLM explainability; conceptual.
notes: in_scope=no (education framework).

title: Generative artificial intelligence in intelligent transportation systems: A systematic review of applications
task: systematic review of generative AI (AIGC) applications in ITS
abstract: Systematic review of generative AI/AIGC technologies (dialogue & reasoning, prediction & decision making, multimodal generation) and applications across ITS subsystems (road, vehicle, traveler, management), plus discussion of safety/ethics risks.
explanation_techniques: mentions dialog/reasoning; not explainability focused
evaluation_design: systematic review
main_findings: Summarizes application areas and highlights risks of fake content and trust/ethics issues.
conclusions: AIGC is increasingly important for ITS but needs safety and governance.
limitations: Not LLM explainability-focused.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no.

title: The potential of generative AI with prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) PET/CT: challenges and future directions
task: review of generative AI for PSMA PET/CT challenges
abstract: Review of clinical challenges in PSMA-PET imaging for prostate cancer and how generative AI methods may help (image enhancement, lesion detection/segmentation, artifact mitigation, data augmentation), noting limited datasets and domain-specific difficulties.
evaluation_design: review
main_findings: Generative AI shows promise for improving PSMA-PET workflows but is early-stage and constrained by data and artifact/uptake complexities.
conclusions: Further datasets, validation, and clinically grounded evaluation are needed for PSMA-PET generative AI tools.
limitations: Not explainability-focused.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=no.

title: A dataset for evaluating clinical research claims in large language models
task: dataset for evaluating LLMs on clinical research claim verification
abstract: Introduces CliniFact, a dataset of clinical research claims derived from clinical trials (interventions, outcomes, p-values) and linked evidence from publications, with labels (evidence/inconclusive/NEI). Evaluates LLMs and discriminative models, finding BioBERT outperforms generative LLMs on claim verification.
explanation_techniques: benchmarking faithfulness/logic; CoT discussed as reasoning aid with faithfulness concerns
evaluation_design: dataset construction + baseline model evaluation
main_findings: CliniFact includes 1,970 instances from 992 trials; BioBERT achieves ~80% accuracy vs Llama3-70B ~54%; highlights hallucination and logical reasoning issues for LLMs.
conclusions: CliniFact enables domain-grounded evaluation of LLM claim verification in clinical research.
limitations: Dataset derived from trials/publications; reasoning evaluation limited to baselines.
notes: in_scope=yes (evaluation benchmark for LLM factuality/claim verification).

title: LLM-as-a-Judge: automated evaluation of search query parsing using large language models
task: LLM-as-a-judge evaluation for query parsing
abstract: Proposes an LLM-as-a-Judge framework to evaluate structured search query parsing outputs via pointwise/pairwise/pass-fail assessments, plus a contextual prompt routing strategy to improve reliability; reports ~90% agreement with human judgments.
explanation_techniques: LLM-based evaluation provides interpretable judgments; prompt routing to reduce hallucinations
evaluation_design: experiments on small and large datasets; comparison to traditional metrics
main_findings: LLM-based evaluation aligns with humans at ~90% and addresses semantic equivalence better than strict match metrics; identifies common issues like over-penalization and lack of semantic awareness in classic metrics.
conclusions: LLM-as-a-Judge can be scalable and semantically aware evaluator for structured outputs in search systems.
limitations: Judge bias and prompt sensitivity remain risks; domain-specific tuning required.
notes: in_scope=partial (evaluation methodology; not explanation generation, but supports reliable assessment).

title: Roadmap on data-centric materials science
venue: Modelling and Simulation in Materials Science and Engineering
task: roadmap for data-centric materials science
abstract: Roadmap paper surveying priorities and directions for data-centric materials science, including infrastructure, standards, and data/ML integration across the field.
evaluation_design: roadmap
main_findings: Outlines challenges and opportunities for data-centric materials research.
conclusions: Calls for coordinated community effort around data and models.
limitations: Not LLM explainability.
notes: in_scope=no.

title: Modelling local and general quantum mechanical properties with attention-based pooling
task: learnable pooling for atom-centered QML models
abstract: Investigates attention-based learnable pooling as a drop-in replacement for sum/mean pooling in atom-centered neural networks for quantum mechanical property prediction, improving performance for localized/intensive properties.
explanation_techniques: attention-based pooling (not XAI; may offer some interpretability signals)
evaluation_design: benchmarking across datasets/properties/levels of theory
main_findings: Consistent performance uplifts vs sum/mean and a physics-aware pooling baseline, with up to 85% improvements on some tasks.
conclusions: Learnable pooling improves representational power for molecular property prediction without changing other model components.
limitations: Not related to LLMs or explainability focus.
notes: in_scope=no (chemistry ML; not LLM explainability).

title: Task-Oriented Semantic Communication in Large Multimodal Models-Based Vehicle Networks
venue: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing (author version / arXiv)
task: task-oriented semantic communication using LMM-based vehicle assistant
abstract: Proposes a task-oriented semantic communication framework for an LMM-based vehicle AI assistant using LLaVA. Optimizes image slicing to focus on user-relevant regions and allocates transmission resources using fused objective+subjective user attention to improve VQA accuracy under bandwidth/SNR constraints.
explanation_techniques: user-attention fusion for prioritizing semantic information (not post-hoc explanations)
evaluation_design: constructed traffic VQA dataset; experiments under varying SNR/channel conditions
main_findings: Improves question-answering accuracy under same channel conditions; gains reported especially at low SNR (e.g., +13.4% at 12dB; +33.1% at 10dB).
conclusions: Task-oriented semantic communication can leverage LMMs and attention to transmit critical information efficiently in vehicle networks.
limitations: Not explainability; relies on LLaVA; evaluation depends on dataset assumptions.
notes: in_scope=no (communication systems; not LLM explainability).

title: Intelligence at play: game-based assessment using a virtual-reality application
task: VR game-based assessment of intelligence
abstract: Proof-of-concept lab study: 103 participants played the VR game Job Simulator and took BIS-4; faster completion correlates with higher general intelligence and processing capacity, suggesting VR games as supplementary assessment tools.
evaluation_design: controlled lab study; correlation/regression
main_findings: Game completion time predicts general intelligence/processing capacity; discusses limitations and future research directions.
conclusions: VR games may supplement personnel assessment but require more validation for criterion-related validity.
limitations: Not LLM/XAI; proof-of-concept only.
notes: in_scope=no.

title: Evaluation of large language model-driven AutoML in data and model management from human-centered perspective
task: evaluate LLM-driven AutoML interface vs traditional ML implementation
abstract: User study (n=15) evaluating an LLM-based AutoML framework with natural language interface for data/model management, reporting higher success/accuracy and reduced development time compared to traditional programming approaches.
explanation_techniques: natural-language interface + guidance; not model explanations
evaluation_design: user study comparing LLM-based vs traditional conditions
main_findings: 93.34% users achieved superior performance in LLM condition; 60% reported substantially reduced development time; reduced error resolution time and faster learning curves reported.
conclusions: Natural language can be effective interface to democratize AutoML and reduce cognitive/implementation barriers.
limitations: Small sample; self-reported measures; depends on task design and system maturity.
notes: in_scope=partial (human-centered evaluation of LLM tooling; not explainability).

title: Explainable Attention Pruning: A Metalearning-Based Approach
venue: IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence (author version / HAL)
task: prune BERT attention heads with meta-learning; evaluate explainability
abstract: Proposes a meta-learning-based structured pruning strategy (MCAP) to identify and remove insignificant BERT attention weights/heads before fine-tuning, improving inference latency while maintaining performance; compares explanations via LIME/SHAP.
explanation_techniques: LIME; SHAP for comparative explanation; attention analysis
evaluation_design: benchmark vs baseline pruning and fine-tuned BERT; XAI comparison
main_findings: Lower inference latency, higher MCC and lower loss than baselines; MCAP attention focuses on more sentiment-relevant words vs baselines; no major FLOPs improvement reported.
conclusions: Meta-learning can produce task-independent pruned PLMs; pruning can also align attention to more salient tokens.
limitations: Not LLM; explainability analysis limited to sentiment task.
notes: in_scope=partial (XAI/attention pruning; not LLM explainability).

title: The Contribution of AI in Climate Modeling and Sustainable Decision-Making
task: overview of AI in climate modeling and decision support
abstract: Overview article discussing AI/ML applications in climate modeling and sustainable decision-making, emphasizing localized prediction models and data availability challenges for high-fidelity simulations.
evaluation_design: narrative overview
main_findings: Argues AI can improve spatiotemporal predictions and support adaptation/mitigation decisions, contingent on data and modeling choices.
conclusions: AI can complement physics-based models for local climate decision support.
limitations: Non-mainstream venue; no empirical evaluation; not LLM/XAI.
notes: in_scope=no.

title: Viewpoint: The Future of Human-Centric Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is not Post-Hoc Explanations
task: viewpoint arguing for interpretable-by-design neural networks vs post-hoc explainers
model: interpretable-by-design workflows (conceptual) + proposes adaptive routing/temporal diagnostics
abstract: Viewpoint argues human-centric XAI should move beyond single post-hoc explainers due to systematic disagreement across methods and calls for intrinsically interpretable neural architectures; identifies five needs (real-time, accurate, actionable, human-interpretable, consistent) and proposes adaptive routing and temporal diagnostics.
explanation_techniques: intrinsic interpretability; adaptive routing; temporal diagnostics; critique of post-hoc explainers
evaluation_design: viewpoint
main_findings: Post-hoc methods are inconsistent and hard to evaluate; interpretable-by-design neural nets may better meet human-centric needs without reverting to classic models.
conclusions: Future human-centric XAI should be intrinsically interpretable deep learning workflows.
limitations: Not LLM-specific; conceptual proposals.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=partial (core XAI argument relevant to survey framing).

title: Practices, opportunities and challenges in the fusion of knowledge graphs and large language models
task: review of strategies and challenges for KG–LLM fusion
abstract: Review of practices and challenges in fusing knowledge graphs and LLMs via three integration strategies (KEL/LEK/LKC), highlighting opportunities for better knowledge representation/reasoning/QA and challenges like knowledge acquisition and real-time updates.
explanation_techniques: KGs offer explainability/traceability; fusion aims to reduce hallucination and improve transparency
evaluation_design: review
main_findings: Categorizes integration approaches and open challenges including KG construction costs, scalability, unstructured integration, and LLM factuality/opacity.
conclusions: KG–LLM fusion is promising for factuality and reasoning but requires robust update and acquisition pipelines.
limitations: Secondary; not specific evaluation metrics.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=partial (KG grounding as explanation mechanism).

title: AI generations: from AI 1.0 to AI 4.0
task: conceptual framework of AI generations (AI 1.0–4.0)
abstract: Proposes a generational framing of AI: AI 1.0 (information AI), AI 2.0 (agentic AI), AI 3.0 (physical AI), and speculative AI 4.0 (conscious AI), discussing drivers (algorithms/compute/data) and governance/ethical implications.
evaluation_design: narrative review / conceptual essay
main_findings: Provides a high-level taxonomy of AI “generations” and discusses anticipated technical and societal challenges as systems become more agentic and autonomous.
conclusions: Framing AI evolution via shifting bottlenecks may help guide research priorities and policy debates.
limitations: Speculative framing (esp. “AI 4.0”); not an empirical study; not focused on explainability in LLMs.
notes: in_scope=no (generic AI evolution; minimal relevance to LLM explainability).

title: Inherently Interpretable Machine Learning: A Contrasting Paradigm to Post-hoc Explainable AI
task: position paper distinguishing inherently interpretable ML vs post-hoc XAI; clarifies terminology and misconceptions
model: inherently interpretable models (e.g., GAM variants, optimal trees) vs black-box + LIME/SHAP
abstract: Catchword article contrasting inherently interpretable machine learning with post-hoc explainable AI; argues confusion in terminology and overreliance on black-boxes; surveys conceptual foundations and implications for decision support.
explanation_techniques: intrinsic interpretability; post-hoc LIME/SHAP as contrasted paradigms
evaluation_design: conceptual/position article
main_findings: Argues interpretability and explainability should be separated; inherently interpretable models can be competitive in structured/tabular settings; warns about inaccuracies/oversimplification of post-hoc explanations.
conclusions: Calls for more nuanced paradigm choice (data type, use case) and stronger BISE/IS contributions to inherently interpretable ML.
limitations: Not LLM-specific; largely conceptual; limited direct coverage of modern LLM explanation methods.
notes: secondary_literature=yes; in_scope=partial (useful framing for survey background; not LLM explainability methods).

title: Beyond phase-in: assessing impacts on disinformation of the EU Digital Services Act
task: research agenda to evaluate Digital Services Act impact on disinformation; methods framework
abstract: Proposes a multi-method framework to empirically evaluate the EU Digital Services Act’s real-world impacts on disinformation, including transparency, auditing, risk assessments, and platform governance changes.
evaluation_design: conceptual research agenda + methodological framework
main_findings: Identifies gaps in DSA mechanisms and outlines quantitative/qualitative/mixed-methods approaches to assess efficacy and unintended consequences.
conclusions: Calls for coordinated efforts and robust empirical measurement to inform regulatory refinement.
limitations: Not focused on LLM explainability; policy-oriented; empirical evidence not provided (agenda).
notes: in_scope=no (policy/disinformation; not LLM explainability).

title: A multimodal symphony: integrating taste and sound through generative AI
task: generate music conditioned on taste descriptions; evaluate taste–sound correspondence
abstract: Explores multimodal generative models that map taste descriptions to music; fine-tunes MusicGEN on taste–affect/music database and evaluates coherence via a user study (n=111).
explanation_techniques: prompt conditioning; not explanation methods
evaluation_design: human-subject evaluation comparing fine-tuned vs baseline model
metrics_performance: coherence of music with taste description (ratings)
main_findings: Fine-tuned model yields music judged more coherent with input taste descriptions than non-fine-tuned baseline.
conclusions: Shows feasibility of crossmodal taste→sound generation and suggests opportunities for embodied multimodal interactions.
limitations: Not LLM explainability; domain-specific; subjective evaluation only.
notes: in_scope=no.

title: INTEGRATING UNIVERSAL GENERATIVE AI PLATFORMS IN EDUCATIONAL LABS TO FOSTER CRITICAL THINKING AND DIGITAL LITERACY
task: pedagogical strategy to foster critical evaluation of LLM outputs in labs
abstract: Presents an interdisciplinary lab model where students test and critique outputs from GenAI platforms (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) to develop critical thinking and digital literacy, implemented in an astronomy course.
explanation_techniques: none (focus on evaluation of outputs, not explanations)
interaction_design: students design prompts/tasks and assess correctness vs known answers
evaluation_design: classroom intervention / descriptive report
main_findings: Argues structured tasks can turn LLM use into critical inquiry and improve subject comprehension and AI literacy.
conclusions: Integrating GenAI as an object of critique may be preferable to bans; highlights need for critical evaluation skills.
limitations: Non-peer-review venue; limited methodological detail and outcomes; not explainability methods.
notes: in_scope=no (education/AI literacy; not LLM explainability).

title: Towards Equipping Transformer with the Ability of Systematic Compositionality
task: improve systematic compositionality in Transformer via CAT and pretraining tasks
abstract: Proposes Compositionality-Aware Transformer (CAT) with modules for multi-primitive composition and representation enhancement plus two pretraining tasks (guided decomposition, semantics composition) to promote systematic compositionality.
explanation_techniques: not XAI; focuses on internal compositional representations/sememes
evaluation_design: benchmark on compositionality-aware tasks + GLUE; robustness/noisy context
metrics_performance: accuracy on compositionality tasks; GLUE; robustness
main_findings: Improves performance on compositionality-aware evaluations with minimal degradation on standard tasks; suggests transformer limitations in compositionality can be mitigated with targeted architecture+pretraining.
conclusions: Proof-of-concept for building models with stronger systematic compositionality, relevant to LLM limitations.
limitations: Not directly about explainability; BERT-scale; compositionality ≠ explanation quality.
notes: in_scope=partial (related to LLM reasoning/compositionality, not explainability methods).

title: A Metaphor for Rethinking Artificial Intelligence in/and Education
venue: Journal of Interactive Media in Education
task: conceptual critique of AI metaphors in education; proposes “mirror” metaphor
abstract: Uses metaphor theory to critique anthropomorphic framings of “AI” in education; proposes the mirror metaphor to expose values/biases and blind spots of systems like LLMs, proctoring, predictive analytics.
explanation_techniques: none (conceptual framing)
evaluation_design: conceptual essay
main_findings: Metaphors shape policy/practice; “mirror” framing highlights hidden assumptions, bias amplification, and what AI systems fail to capture in education.
conclusions: Calls for reimagining education’s purposes and critical pedagogy rather than reactive technical fixes.
limitations: Not empirical; not about explainability methods.
notes: in_scope=no.

title: Manifestations of xenophobia in AI systems
domain: AI ethics / fairness / bias in large models
task: framework of xenophobic harms and how they manifest across AI domains including large pre-trained models
abstract: Defines xenophobia and distinct xenophobic harms, surveys manifestations across AI domains (social media, healthcare, immigration, employment) including biases in large pre-trained models, and recommends inclusive “xenophilic” design.
evaluation_design: review + normative argument
main_findings: Argues xenophobia is under-addressed in fairness frameworks; proposes harm typology and design recommendations; highlights challenges in detecting/mitigating xenophobic harms.

title: Interacting with fallible AI: is distrust helpful when receiving AI misclassifications?
task: test whether “remain skeptical” instruction improves performance when AI advice becomes unreliable
abstract: Human-AI interaction study on trust and distrust: participants receive mock AI classification advice whose quality decreases mid-experiment; compares a skepticism instruction vs neutral; measures performance and self-reported trust/distrust; includes Bayesian Signal Detection Theory for reliance assessment.
explanation_techniques: disclaimer/instruction framing; reliance analysis via (Bayesian) signal detection theory
interaction_design: participants receive AI advice; instructed skeptical vs neutral; repeated trust/distrust probes
evaluation_design: controlled experiment with advice quality drop; Bayesian SDT reliance analysis
main_findings: Instruction to remain skeptical worsened performance; trust increased and distrust decreased after advice quality dropped; proposes Bayesian SDT procedure to quantify under-/over-reliance.
conclusions: Simple disclaimers/instructions may not mitigate overreliance; measuring distrust separately can clarify appropriate reliance; relevance to LLM chatbot disclaimers discussed.
limitations: Not LLM explainability methods; uses mock advisor; task is image classification; generalization to real systems limited.
notes: in_scope=partial (human evaluation of trust/distrust + reliance; useful for XAI/LLM deployment discussion, not methods).

title: An in-depth analysis of passage-level label transfer for contextual document ranking
task: passage-level label transfer for long-document ranking with contextual LMs
model: BERT-based rankers; external QA/passage ranker for weak supervision
abstract: Study of design decisions for splitting long documents into passages and transferring relevance labels for BERT-style document ranking; finds direct doc→passage label transfer introduces harmful label noise; proposes weak-supervision passage labeling using an external QA/passage ranker improving nDCG (reported 3–14%) with manageable compute.
evaluation_design: benchmark experiments across multiple retrieval datasets; efficiency–effectiveness analysis
main_findings: Direct document-level labels induce noisy passage supervision (worse with large training sets); finer splitting increases processing time; weak-supervision passage labels improve ranking while controlling compute.
conclusions: Careful passage labeling is key for contextual rerankers under token limits; external rankers can provide useful distant supervision.
limitations: Not LLM explainability; IR-specific; relies on external model labeling.
